State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804, • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 17, 1976

A-96-477

Mr. Charles Schwartz
Professor of Physics
University of California, Berkeley
Berkeley, California 94720

Dear Mr. Schwartz:

The Executive Director has asked me to respond to your
letter of June 8 concerning the University's development of
a Conflict of Interest Code.

There isn't much I can add to Mr. Reidhaar's letter of
April 13. It is my understanding that the University intends
to hold a public hearing, properly noticed, to discuss the
adoption of its code. Mr. Reidhaar also appears to concur in
Chairman Lowenstein's general remarks concerning the inclusion
or exclusion of faculty members from the requirements of the
Code. Government Code § 87302 states:

(a) Specific enumeration of the positions
within the agency which involve the making
or participation in the making of decisions
which may foreseeably have a material effect
on any financial interest;

(b) Requirements that each designated
employee file annual statements disclosing
reportable investments, interest in real
property and income. The Conflict of
Interest Code shall set forth for each
position or category of positions enumerated
under subsection (a) of this section the
specific types of investments, interests in
real property and income which are reportable and the manner of reporting each item....

As long as a Conflict of Interest Code adequately fulfills
these requirements, the Fair Political Practices Commission will
not substitute its judgment for that of the Agency's as to questions of designation of employees.

The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with only some faint marks or artifacts visible at the top. There is no clearly legible text content to transcribe.

Mr. Charles Schwartz                                June 17, 1976
Page Two


     Assuming that the University's Code is received on or
about August 10, the Fair Political Practices Commission's
hearing on the approval of the draft Code will probably
take place in Sacramento sometime in mid-September. We will
inform you directly of that date.

     I hope this answers your questions. If I can be of
further assistance, please do not hesitate to call on me
at (916) 322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [illegible]
                                        Kenneth W. Goshorn
                                        Research Specialist
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

KWG:ma

UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, BERKELEY

BERKELEY • DAVIS • IRVINE • LOS ANGELES • RIVERSIDE • SAN DIEGO • SAN FRANCISCO                    SANTA BARBARA • SANTA CRUZ

DEPARTMENT OF PHYSICS                                         BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA   94720

June 8, 1976

Mr. Michael Bennett, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
Sacramento, California

Dear Mr. Bennett;

I wish to bring to your attention the enclosed correspondence,
which concerns the response of the University of California to the
Political Reform Act of 1974.

It appears that the officials of this University - an extremely
important institution in California - have made very little effort
to meet their legal requirements for consultation in the development of
their Conflict of Interest Code.

My own concern is twofold: both as a private citizen and as an
employee of the University I have an interest in the University's
Code.  I would appreciate any advice you can give me at this time; and
I would like to be kept informed of any upcoming proceedings of the
FPPC that deal with the University.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Charles Schwartz
                                        Professor of Physics


Enclosed:  Letter of March 28, 1976, from Schwartz to Reidhaar
           Letter of April 13, 1976, from Reidhaar to Schwartz
           Letter of June 8, 1976, from Schwartz to Reidhaar

[Several lines of heavily faded/illegible text in upper portion]

McConnell on 26 February said he believed the State enactment of
[illegible] state plan would [illegible] in substantial inaction.
In measurement of his consequences the following case is posed in
[illegible] [illegible] [illegible]

In U.S. Bank of the District, I am asked whether it would be
justified for me to invest in Connecticut bonds that are then
[illegible] sold in an open the are equal [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]
act be redeemed pursuant the [illegible] [illegible] at still other
[illegible] [illegible] of acts [illegible] of [illegible]

BERKELEY: DEPARTMENT OF PHYSICS

March 23, 1976


Donald L. Reidhaar
General Counsel to the Regents
590 University Hall
Berkeley Campus

Dear Mr. Reidhaar:

I have some questions concerning the University's response to the
requirements of the Political Reform Act of 1974 ("Proposition 9").

1. I would like to receive a copy of the University's draft Conflict of
Interest Code, whomever this document is available. If it will not be
available by April 1, please advise me when you expect it to be ready.

2. When and where is the University planning to hold the meetings, to
discuss this draft Code, announced in the February 2, 1976, issue of the
University Bulletin ?

3. In that February 2 issue of the Bulletin it also states, "It now appears
that the University's Conflict of Interest Code will need to require financial
disclosure statements of most nonclerkical university personnel including
faculty." However, at a recent public meeting on this campus I had
the opportunity of asking Daniel Lowenstein, chairman of the state's Fair
Political Practices Commission which must approve the University's Code,
about this assertion. He replied that this was probably not true; he thought
that most faculty would not be covered by the financial disclosure requirement
- that this requirement would be limited to "those heavily into administration"
and those involved in certain kinds of research projects involving particular
economic interests. I would like to know your present thoughts on this point.

Sincerely,

Charles Schwartz
Professor of Physics


UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA - (Letterhead for inter-departmental use)

I can see this appears to be a mostly blank page with some very faint marks or text at the top that are not clearly legible in this image. The page appears to be predominantly white space with minimal visible content.

[illegible]

OFFICE OF THE GENERAL COUNSEL
University Hall
BERKELEY—EXTENSION 2-2532

April 13, 1976

PROFESSOR CHARLES L. SCHWARTZ
Berkeley

Re: Conflict of Interest Code

Dear Professor Schwartz:

This will respond to your March 28, 1976 request
for information concerning the Conflict of Interest Code
being developed by the University pursuant to the Political
Reform Act of 1974 (Proposition 9).

You first ask for a copy of the University's draft
Conflict of Interest Code. We have, of course, given considerable thought to the requirements of Proposition 9, but
have not as yet developed a draft of the Code. The Fair
Political Practices Commission (FPPC) has requested that the
University's Code be submitted by August 10, 1976 and a
draft Code will be available for comment before submission
to the FPPC.

You next ask when we plan to hold meetings to
discuss the draft Code. Government Code Section 87311
requires that the preparation of proposed Conflict of Interest
Codes by state agencies is subject to the Administrative
Procedure Act, which requires giving members of the public
an opportunity to comment. The Administrative Procedure Act
does not, by its terms, apply to the University, but our
present intent is to develop procedures analogous to those
required by the Administrative Procedure Act. Our present
plan would be to submit the Code to The Regents in July and
to provide the opportunity to comment before that time.

Finally you ask for my present thoughts on the
question of whether faculty members will be covered by the
financial disclosure requirements of Proposition 9. I am
hopeful that it will be determined that faculty positions
as such do not involve the making of decisions foreseeably
having a material effect on financial interests. If so,
faculty members in general would not be required to file
disclosure statements.

[signed]
Donald L. Reidhaar
General Counsel

cc: Chancellor Bowker

UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA—(Letterhead for interdepartmental use)

I can see this appears to be a mostly blank page with some very faint marks or artifacts at the top (possibly page numbers or scanning artifacts) and a few small dots scattered on the page, but no clearly legible text content to transcribe.

BERKELEY: DEPARTMENT OF PHYSICS

June 8, 1976

Donald L. Reidhaar
General Counsel to the Regents
590 University Hall
Berkeley Campus

Dear Mr. Reidhaar:

This letter is a continuation of our earlier correspondence
regarding the University's Conflict of Interest Code required
under the Political Reform Act of 1974.

In your letter of April 13, 1976, you state, regarding your timetable,
that you plan
a) to submit the Draft Code to the FPPC by August 10, 1976,
b) to submit the Draft Code to the Regents in July,
c) to provide the opportunity for comments on the Draft Code before then.

As of this date, I have heard no announcement inviting members of
the University community or the general public to inspect your Draft Code,
meet and discuss the Draft Code, and submit comments on it. Indeed, I
am still waiting to receive a copy of your Draft Code for myself, which
I requested in my letter of March 28, 1976.

This is the last week of classes on this campus: students will be
gone shortly and many faculty members as well will be away for the
summer. Thus, I do not see how you can possibly meet with any fairness
the requirements for providing adequate opportunity for public review
and comment regarding the Draft Code.

I am sending a copy of our correspondence on this matter to
the FPPC.

Sincerely,

Charles Schwartz
Professor of Physics

UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA — (Letterhead for interdepartmental use)
